title: LLM-HGAN: LLM-Enhanced Heterogeneous Graph Attention Networks for Advanced Persistent Threat Detection
authors: Kun Lan, Gaolei Li, Wenkai Huang, Jianhua Li, Yantao Yu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Network Science and Engineering
abstract: Advanced Persistent Threats (APTs) have evolved into highly stealthy and organized cyber attacks, posing one of the most severe challenges to global cyber security. However, existing detection methods often suffer from high false positive rates and explosion of provenance graphs, making it difficult to counter sophisticated evasion techniques, diverse attack stages, and long latency periods. To address these challenges, this article proposes LLM-HGAN, a two-stage APT detection framework that combines heterogeneous graph attention networks (HGAN) with large language models (LLMs). Specifically, suspicious processes are first identified by learning similarity on heterogeneous provenance graphs using LLM-HGAN, enabling the detection of subtle behavioral deviations across complex attack surfaces. Then, the detected anomalies are correlated with candidate attack paths and verified against a curated TTP (tactics, techniques, and procedures) rule base. Finally, LLMs operate in parallel to generate, normalize, and continuously update TTP rules from threat intelligence and system telemetry, ensuring that the detection process remains adaptive to evolving attacker behaviors. Extensive experiments on a hybrid dataset that combines curated APT samples from five public repositories with a high-fidelity emulated APT scenario corpus show that LLM-HGAN achieves 95% detection accuracy, surpasses state-of-the-art baselines by at least 14%, and reaches 98% accuracy on real-world APT variants, demonstrating strong robustness and generalization to unknown-vulnerability attacks.

title: LLM-Driven Ontology Construction for Enterprise Knowledge Graphs
authors: Abdulsobur Oyewale, Tommaso Soru
abstract: Enterprise Knowledge Graphs have become essential for unifying heterogeneous data and enforcing semantic governance. However, the construction of their underlying ontologies remains a resource-intensive, manual process that relies heavily on domain expertise. This paper introduces OntoEKG, a LLM-driven pipeline designed to accelerate the generation of domain-specific ontologies from unstructured enterprise data. Our approach decomposes the modelling task into two distinct phases: an extraction module that identifies core classes and properties, and an entailment module that logically structures these elements into a hierarchy before serialising them into standard RDF. Addressing the significant lack of comprehensive benchmarks for end-to-end ontology construction, we adopt a new evaluation dataset derived from documents across the Data, Finance, and Logistics sectors. Experimental results highlight both the potential and the challenges of this approach, achieving a fuzzy-match F1-score of 0.724 in the Data domain while revealing limitations in scope definition and hierarchical reasoning.

title: Vibe Coding on Trial: Operating Characteristics of Unanimous LLM Juries
authors: Muhammad Aziz Ullah, Abdul Serwadda
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are now good enough at coding that developers can describe intent in plain language and let the tool produce the first code draft, a workflow increasingly built into tools like GitHub Copilot, Cursor, and Replit. What is missing is a reliable way to tell which model written queries are safe to accept without sending everything to a human. We study the application of an LLM jury to run this review step. We first benchmark 15 open models on 82 MySQL text to SQL tasks using an execution grounded protocol to get a clean baseline of which models are strong. From the six best models we build unanimous committees of sizes 1 through 6 that see the prompt, schema, and candidate SQL and accept it only when every member says it is correct. This rule matches safety first deployments where false accepts are more costly than false rejects. We measure true positive rate, false positive rate and Youden J and we also look at committees per generator. Our results show that single model judges are uneven, that small unanimous committees of strong models can cut false accepts while still passing many good queries, and that the exact committee composition matters significantly.

title: Domain-Aware Hallucination Characterization and Mitigation in LLM-Generated SQL
authors: Preet Saumil Merchant, David Alfred Ostrowski
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) present challenges in validation, particularly in light of hallucinogenic output. This work examines hallucinations in structured analytical domains, where output correctness is deterministically verifiable. This paper concentrates on LLM-generated SQL, analyzing hallucinations as structured constraint violations rather than purely stochastic errors. An initial taxonomy of domain-specific hallucination types is applied, including schema fabrication, logical aggregation errors, and constraint violations. The goal in this strategy is to examine their persistence across models of varying sizes. Preliminary observations suggest diminishing returns from model scaling alone in reducing these errors.

title: Multilingual ZPD-Driven Personalized L2 Generation via Hybrid LLM Prompting
authors: Jingyang Wang
publish: 2025 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Electrical and Electronic Engineering (AI3E)
abstract: Personalized adaptation of second language (L2) content remains challenging due to the neglect of L1-mediated influences and the dynamic nature of the Zone of Proximal Development (ZPD) in existing rule-based or static-threshold methods. To address this, a novel framework is proposed for dynamically adapting $L 2$ content by integrating linguistic and cognitive factors into the ZPD through hybrid prompt engineering with large language models (LLMs). The Multilingual ZPD-Driven Adaptation Module (MZPD-AM) parameterizes ZPD boundaries across lexical, syntactic, and discourse dimensions and synthesizes constrained LLM prompts for personalized content generation. It incorporates a transformer-based ZPD Profiler for adaptive difficulty estimation, a Prompt Strategist for real-time constraint generation, and a Dynamic Calibrator for iterative optimization based on learner performance. Experiments on the L1-L2 dataset demonstrate significant improvements in comprehension accuracy and learner engagement across diverse L1-L2 pairs, offering a scalable solution for personalized L2 content generation that bridges linguistic neurolinguistics with LLM prompt engineering.

title: LLM-Enhanced Reinforcement Learning for Time Series Anomaly Detection
authors: Bahareh Golchin, Banafsheh Rekabdar, Danielle Justo
abstract: Detecting anomalies in time series data is crucial for finance, healthcare, sensor networks, and industrial monitoring applications. However, time series anomaly detection often suffers from sparse labels, complex temporal patterns, and costly expert annotation. We propose a unified framework that integrates Large Language Model (LLM)-based potential functions for reward shaping with reinforcement learning (RL), Variational Autoencoder (VAE)-enhanced dynamic reward scaling, and active learning with label propagation. An LSTM-based RL agent leverages LLM-derived semantic rewards to guide exploration, while VAE reconstruction errors add unsupervised anomaly signals. Active learning selects the most uncertain samples, and label propagation expands labeled data efficiently. Evaluations on Yahoo-A1 and SMD benchmarks demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art detection accuracy under limited labeling budgets and operates effectively in data-constrained settings. This study highlights the promise of combining LLMs with RL and advanced unsupervised techniques for robust, scalable anomaly detection in real-world applications.<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sup>

title: SIAgent: Spatial Interaction Agent Via LLM-Powered Eye-Hand Motion Intent Understanding in VR_supp1-3686395.mp4
authors: Feng Lu
abstract: Eye-hand coordinated interaction is becoming a mainstream interaction modality in Virtual Reality (VR) user interfaces. Current paradigms for this multimodal interaction require users to learn predefined gestures and memorize multiple gesture-task associations, which can be summarized as an “Operation-to-Intent” paradigm. This paradigm increases users' learning costs and has low interaction error tolerance. In this paper, we propose SIAgent, a novel “Intent-to-Operation” framework allowing users to express interaction intents through natural eye-hand motions based on common sense and habits. Our system features two main components: (1) intent recognition that translates spatial interaction data into natural language and infers user intent, and (2) agent-based execution that generates an agent to execute corresponding tasks. This eliminates the need for gesture memorization and accommodates individual motion preferences with high error tolerance. We conduct two user studies across over 60 interaction tasks, comparing our method with two “Operation-to-Intent” techniques. Results show our method achieves higher intent recognition accuracy than gaze + pinch interaction (97.2% vs 93.1%) while reducing arm fatigue and improving usability, and user preference. Another study verifies the function of eye gaze and hand motion channels in intent recognition. Our work offers valuable insights into enhancing VR interaction intelligence through intent-driven design. Our source code and LLM prompts will be made available upon publication. Our project page is at https://zhimin-wang.github.io/SIAgent.html

title: LLM-Augmented Semantic Integration: Scalable Knowledge Graph Construction from Unstructured Safety-Critical Narratives
authors: Wasiu Sanyaolu, Darsana P. Josyula
abstract: The integration of domain-specific narratives or unstructured data into structured semantic representations is a critical challenge in infrastructure safety and decision support. This paper presents an end-to-end pipeline that constructs domain-specific knowledge graphs (KGs) from unstructured accident reports using large language models (LLMs) for semantic enrichment, schema-constrained entity-relation extraction, and graph machine learning for prediction of risks. We collated 6,193 National Transportation Safety Board (NTSB) Aviation Investigation Final Reports, we processed over 6,000 pages of text extracting triples spanning accident attributes, aircraft configuration, human and environmental factors, and causal chains. We achieve an F1 score of 0.87 on manually annotated extractions across eight semantic categories. Our approach is based on schema-guided graph construction that aligns with domain ontologies. We further apply graph convolutional networks (GCNs) for accident risk prediction, achieving $82.5 \%$ accuracy in classifying severity categories. The constructed knowledge graph supports transparent reasoning over latent factors contributing to accident outcomes. We also highlight the adopted mitigation strategies for knowledge conflicts and biases inherent in historical narratives by incorporating provenance tracking, contradiction detection heuristics. The resulting knowledge graph enables semantic querying and causal path analysis that serves as a reusable substrate for infrastructure-level safety monitoring. This work demonstrates how generative AI, grounded in symbolic constraints and semantic schemas, can drive scalable and reliable knowledge integration in data-fragmented environments.

title: Prompt Sentiment: The Catalyst for LLM Change
authors: Vishal Gandhi, Sagar Gandhi
publish: Proceedings of the 2025 9th International Conference on Advances in Artificial Intelligence
abstract: None

title: LLM-based Zero-shot Triple Extraction for Automated Ontology Generation from Software Engineering Standards
authors: Songhui Yue
abstract: Ontologies have supported knowledge representation and white-box reasoning for decades; thus, the automated ontology generation (AOG) plays a crucial role in scaling their use. Software engineering standards (SES) consist of long, unstructured text (with high noise) and paragraphs with domain-specific terms. In this setting, relation triple extraction (RTE), together with term extraction, constitutes the first stage toward AOG. This work proposes an open-source large language model (LLM)-assisted approach to RTE for SES. Instead of solely relying on prompt-engineering-based methods, this study promotes the use of LLMs as an aid in constructing ontologies and explores an effective AOG workflow that includes document segmentation, candidate term mining, LLM-based relation inference, term normalization, and cross-section alignment. Expert-annotated reference sets at three granularities are constructed and used to evaluate the ontology generated from the study. The results show that it is comparable and potentially superior to the OpenIE method of triple extraction.

title: Deep Reinforcement Adaptive Learning with LLM Enrichment for Cybersecurity Ontology Alignment
authors: Mehrnoosh Zaeifi, Faezeh Kalantari, Mehdi Zaeifi, Srividya Bansal
abstract: This paper investigates the effectiveness of the Deep Reinforcement Adaptive Learning for Ontology Alignment (DRALOA) framework in aligning cybersecurity ontologies. Given the domain’s complex terminology and evolving structure, we evaluate whether DRAL-OA performs adequately and explore how its performance can be enhanced. We compare three alignment strategies: (1) a baseline using general-purpose BERT embeddings, (2) four cybersecurity-specific embeddings (CyBERT, CTI-BERT, CySecBERT, SecureBERT), and (3) LLMenriched pipelines that pair these embeddings with zero-shot large language models, including ChatGPT 4.1, ChatGPT 4o, their mini variants, and LLaMA models. Experiments on two well-established cybersecurity ontologies assess alignment performance using precision, recall, and F-measure at Top-1, Top-3, and Top-5 levels. Among embedding-only methods, CTI-BERT yields the highest performance, while only ChatGPT 4.1 and ChatGPT 4o consistently improve alignment across all thresholds. These high-capacity LLMs also close the gap between BERT and CTI-BERT, enabling general-purpose models to achieve domain-specific performance. Our findings reveal that DRALOA is effective in cybersecurity settings but can be substantially improved through LLM enrichment, making it a more adaptable and domain-agnostic solution for ontology alignment.

title: Hierarchical Sparse Plus Low Rank Compression of LLM
authors: pawan kumar, Aditi Gupta
publish: Proceedings of the 13th ACM IKDD International Conference on Data Science
abstract: None

title: Faithful or Plausible? A Counterfactual Analysis of LLM-Generated Explanations for Machine Learning Models
authors: Shaun Colegado, Jennifer Jin, Yunfei Hou
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used to generate natural language explanations of predictive machine learning models, yet their outputs often emphasize plausibility over faithfulness to the underlying model logic. This study applies counterfactual testing to evaluate the fidelity of LLM explanations in a structured setting: decision tree models predicting customer churn (i.e., when customers stop using a company’s product or service-a classic benchmark problem in predictive modeling research). We design two counterfactual tests across four prompting strategies that vary in whether dataset and/or model information are provided. Results from 1,200 generated explanations show that supplying model context substantially improves faithfulness whereas dataset context provides little benefit, and LLMs often broaden explanations beyond the model’s true decision path. Notably, even when given full model logic, LLMs remain predisposed toward generating plausible rather than strictly faithful explanations. These findings highlight both the promise and the limits of LLMs as interpreters of predictive models and introduce counterfactual testing as a potential framework for evaluating explanation faithfulness in data-heavy contexts.

title: Dual-Population Cooperative Algorithm Integrating an LLM and an Evolutionary Algorithm for AUV Path Planning in Three-Dimensional Marine Environments_supp1-3686625.docx
authors: Zhiyong Wang
abstract: Existing AUV path planning methods still face limitations in modeling real three-dimensional (3D) maritime search and rescue (SAR) environments, multi-objective evaluation and safety constraint expression, and maintaining convergence stability in complex search spaces. To address these issues, we propose a dual-population cooperative algorithm that integrates a large language model (LLM) with an evolutionary algorithm (EA) for marine SAR tasks, named LLM_EA. Firstly, we integrate real seabed topography and 3D ocean currents to construct a unified navigation environment. A multi-objective evaluation framework is further designed to balance energy consumption, safety risk, and attitude stability. During the evolutionary process, LLM_EA performs parallel co-evolution between an EA population for numerical search and an LLM population for semantic reconstruction. A feasibility assurance and cross-population information exchange mechanism is designed to improve feasibility and efficiency, and a topological diversity preservation mechanism is introduced to mitigate topological collapse and maintain structural diversity. Comparative experiments show that LLM_EA consistently generates feasible trajectories under diverse geographical environments and complex constraints while achieving better overall performance and more stable convergence than multiple representative algorithms. Ablation experiments further confirm the contributions of the topological diversity preservation mechanism and the LLM strategy. This framework offers a reusable solution for efficient and robust AUV path planning in real-world 3D maritime SAR scenarios.

title: Edge-Deployed Conversational LLM Agents for Semantic Service Matching
authors: Jonas Scheffler, Guido Dartmann, Alexander Koß, Stephan Multhaupt, Lejla Begic Fazlic
abstract: Rural communities often face limited access to digital services due to infrastructural constraints and lower levels of digital literacy. Conventional form-based platforms require precise inputs and predefined categories, creating barriers for users unfamiliar with structured digital systems. This work presents a conversational platform that leverages a Large Language Model (LLM) agent to connect citizens with local companies through natural dialogue. The system integrates a conversational agent with tool-calling capabilities, a retrieval pipeline over a curated company database, and lightweight backend and web components. To ensure accessibility and sustainability, it employs open-source, quantized LLMs deployable on edge hardware. Evaluation across 260 representative user queries shows that compact 4-bit models, such as Qwen3 8B, achieve high decision accuracy in tool invocation as well as parameter extraction accuracy while maintaining feasible decoding speed and energy efficiency on an NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano Super. These findings demonstrate that small open-source LLMs can support inclusive, low-power conversational services, reducing reliance on centralized infrastructure and advancing digital inclusion in rural contexts.

title: Development of Hybrid LLM-ASR Methodology for Improving Process of Kazakh Language Learning
authors: Dinmukhammed Zhassulanov, Azamat Serek, Aisaule Bazarkulova
publish: 2026 18th International Conference on Electronics, Computer, and Computation (ICECCO)
abstract: This paper suggests a structure that can guide the enhancement of the Kazakh language learning process by using the large language models (LLMs) and automatic speech recognition (ASR). To fill the gap of highquality speech materials on the Kazakh language, a custom dataset consisting of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{3, 4 8 2}$</tex> anonymized audio-text pairs was collected among native speakers of the language in different locations and in different recording conditions. This dataset has a vast variety of accents, speaking styles, and conditions, and thus, it is an excellent starting point when it comes to model training. It reduced the Word Error Rate (WER) according to epochs of Whisper-small of OpenAI (from 87% to 32.7) by fine tuning on this corpus. This demonstrates the effectiveness of transfer learning in language environments with low resources. The system was tested in a classroom with 180 students, in terms of its effectiveness in learning. In a survey taken after the deployment, 87 % of the learners believed that their pronunciation skills had improved and 91 % believed that they were now more motivated to do exercises with AI assistance. These findings suggest that the proposed system can enhance the process of training pronunciation, motivation to learn, and individualized training. The study also examines the ways of utilizing the mobile devices in the way they can provide adaptive real time feedback. Code-switching, data not being sufficiently available, and devices having performance constraints are some of the issues that remain an unresolved problem. The data augmentation, model compression, and grammar correction with the help of LLM will be investigated by us in the future to simplify and improve the learning process. The study indicates that language learning can significantly enhance the efficiency and expansiveness of fine-tuning custom ASR using LLM evaluation in languages that lack assets.

title: Bridging Hardware and Semantics: An LLM-Enhanced Ontology Framework for Explainable Anomaly Detection in IoMT Systems
authors: Thrity Golzari, Zhangying He, Hossein Sayadi
abstract: This paper presents an LLM-enhanced ontology framework for cybersecurity in Internet of Medical Things (IoMT) environments, integrating hardware performance counter (HPC) features, device metadata, and known vulnerabilities to enable reasoning-driven, explainable anomaly detection. The study introduces a hybrid human-LLM ontology development workflow that accelerates ontology construction and refinement while preserving conceptual rigor and semantic consistency. It further investigates how large language models (LLMs) can augment ontology engineering through a comparative evaluation of three state-of-the-art models: ChatGPT-o3, ChatGPT-4o, and Gemini 2.5 Pro, assessed across structural and semantic dimensions. Using the proposed LLM-enhanced ontology, we achieve 84.29% anomaly (e.g., malware) classification accuracy from HPC data while mapping detected anomalies to relevant vulnerabilities and device properties. Experimental results demonstrate that LLMs significantly improve ontology quality and reasoning coherence, with Gemini 2.5 Pro achieving the best balance between expansion and consistency.

title: EM³R: An Explainable Modular Multi-LLM Reasoning Framework for Question Answering System
authors: Siwen Zhao, Sen Lin, Wei Wang, Yujun Li
publish: 2026 9th International Symposium on Big Data and Applied Statistics (ISBDAS)
abstract: Entailment tree generation provides a structured paradigm for explainable question answering by explicitly modeling multi-step logical inference. However, most existing large language model (LLM)-based approaches rely on a single model and treat reasoning as an unconstrained generation process, resulting in limited controllability over intermediate inference steps. As a result, early reasoning errors readily propagate across steps, while large search spaces induced by distractor facts hinder reliable entailment tree construction. To address these challenges, we propose the Explainable Modular Multi-LLM Reasoning framework (EM<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup>R), which reframes entailment tree construction as an interactive, verifier-guided reasoning process. EM<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup>R follows a stepwise Select–Decompose–Propose–Verify workflow that explicitly separates evidence selection, goal planning, step generation, and entailment validation. Specifically, a Semantic Selector filters hypothesis-relevant facts, a Goal Decomposer derives intermediate subgoals, a Step Proposer generates executable entailment steps, and an Entailment Verifier validates each step before it is incorporated into the proof. Verified steps are then assembled into a structured and human-interpretable entailment tree. Experiments on the EntailmentBank benchmark show that EM<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup>R produces more coherent and structurally consistent entailment trees than strong baselines. Under the strict AllCorrect evaluation, EM<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup>R achieves a 44.1% improvement in overall accuracy, indicating substantially improved global reasoning correctness. These results demonstrate that explicitly controlling search and validating intermediate reasoning steps is critical for reliable and interpretable multi-step question answering.
